title: Why Mayfair’s BiBi Lates is worth staying out for
summary:
London restaurant BiBi has introduced a new late-night format, BiBi Lates, giving guests the chance to experience signature dishes alongside an expanded cocktail offering after dinner service.

The post Why Mayfair’s BiBi Lates is worth staying out for appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
London restaurant BiBi has introduced a new late-night format, BiBi Lates, giving guests the chance to experience signature dishes alongside an expanded cocktail offering after dinner service.

Award-winning London restaurant BiBi has launched BiBi Lates, a new evening concept designed to extend service beyond its regular dinner offering.

Available from 9pm onwards, the new format pairs an extended cocktail list with an à la carte menu featuring a selection of dishes from the restaurant's tasting menu.

Signature dishes served after dark

Guests visiting BiBi Lates can order dishes including Mangalori Scallops and Launceston Lamb Barra Kebab, offering a more flexible way to experience chef Chet Sharma's cooking without committing to the full tasting menu.

The restaurant said the new format is designed to allow diners to enjoy BiBi's atmosphere later into the evening while sampling a curated selection of its signature dishes.

BiBi takes its name from the South Asian word that formally means "lady of the house", although Sharma has adopted its colloquial Urdu meaning of "grandma" as a tribute to his two grandmothers, whose influence is woven throughout the restaurant.

Research-led approach

Opened in September 2021, BiBi's menus are underpinned by years of research into ingredients, with an emphasis on traceability and ethics. The restaurant sources dayboat-landed fish from British waters, hand-dived scallops from Devon, buffalo milk from Lancashire and cheese from Somerset, while using heritage rice varieties and single-source spices from the Indian subcontinent.

The kitchen also follows sustainability and zero-waste practices, including composting, recycling, fermentation, preservation and maximising the use of individual ingredients.

Alongside its food offering, BiBi serves a wine list designed to complement the menus, as well as reinvented classic cocktails, non-alcoholic juices and cold-brewed teas. The sommelier team, led by Wilem Powell, offers two wine pairings inspired by Sharma's grandfathers.

Since opening, the Mayfair restaurant has received a number of industry accolades, including Best Cocktail List of the Year at the National Restaurant Awards 2025.

title: Vintae completes Bodegas Riojanas acquisition
summary:
Vintae has completed its acquisition of Bodegas Riojanas after the registration of the winery's restructuring plan, giving the Rioja-based wine group a 90% stake in the historic producer.

The post Vintae completes Bodegas Riojanas acquisition appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Vintae has completed its acquisition of Bodegas Riojanas after the registration of the winery's restructuring plan, giving the Rioja-based wine group a 90% stake in the historic producer.

Vintae has completed its acquisition of Bodegas Riojanas after the registration of the winery's Restructuring Plan with the Commercial Registry, formalising the entry of its holding company, Gevisa Wine Capital, as the majority shareholder.

The registration follows approval by Commercial Court No. 6 of Logroño and completes a process that began in May. Bodegas Riojanas will retain its status as a publicly listed company, with its shares continuing to trade on the Spanish continuous market.

Under the Restructuring Plan, Gevisa Wine Capital has acquired a 90% stake in Bodegas Riojanas through a capital reduction followed by a simultaneous capital increase via the capitalisation of debt.

The transaction represents a €12 million investment by Gevisa Wine Capital, following an agreement with the financial institutions that adhered to the Restructuring Plan. Vintae will also provide Bodegas Riojanas with a financing facility of up to €5 million to support the next phase of the business.

Board changes

The registration of the Restructuring Plan also brings changes to the board of Bodegas Riojanas.

Ricardo Arambarri Pérez has been appointed chairman, while Fernando Martínez Sampedro becomes chief executive officer. Isabel García Castellanos and Raúl Acha Salazar have joined the board as proprietary directors.

The company said two independent directors will be appointed in September in line with regulations governing publicly listed companies.

The deal has now entered its final administrative phase, which involves the admission to trading of the newly issued shares before the Spanish National Securities Market Commission (CNMV). According to the company, this process will not affect either the effectiveness of the transaction or the day-to-day operations of Bodegas Riojanas.

Expanding Vintae's portfolio

The acquisition adds several wineries and assets to the Vintae portfolio, including Bodegas Riojanas in Rioja, Bodegas Torreduero in Toro, Bodegas Viore in Rueda and a 56% stake in Veiga Naúm in Rías Baixas, together with their production assets, vineyards, facilities and commercial brands.

Founded in 1890, Bodegas Riojanas owns brands including Monte Real and Viña Albina. Vintae said the transaction marks the beginning of a new chapter for the winery, supported by a strengthened financial structure and the backing of a group with a commercial presence in Spain and more than 70 countries worldwide.

The company added that it will define Bodegas Riojanas' medium- and long-term development plan while reviewing the positioning of the group's companies and brands and gradually integrating processes, systems and management models.

Richi Arambarri, CEO of Vintae, said: "This transaction represents another important step in Vintae's evolution. Over the past few years, we have built a company with projects that are deeply rooted in their territories, supported by a solid business structure and strong commercial capabilities. That journey is what now enables us to take on a project of this scale with confidence and responsibility."

He added: "We begin this new chapter with the utmost respect for the identity of Bodegas Riojanas and with the conviction that we can help drive its future while preserving the value of its history and its brands."

title: Tim Atkin MW elected as chair of Grand Orden de Caballeros del Vino
summary:
Tim Atkin MW has been elected as the new chair of the Gran Orden de Caballeros del Vino Español, replacing Sarah Jane Evans MW, who has held the position since 2020.  

The post Tim Atkin MW elected as chair of Grand Orden de Caballeros del Vino  appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Tim Atkin MW has been elected as the new chair of the Gran Orden de Caballeros del Vino Español, replacing Sarah Jane Evans MW, who has held the position since 2020.   Wine writer Tim Atkin MW has been elected by the members of the Gran Orden de Caballeros del Vino (GOCV) as the new Chair of the Order, which was established in the UK in 1984 to recognise individuals in the wine sector who have shown exceptional endeavour in promoting Spanish wines.  Today its members come from all aspects of the world of wine, in the UK and Spain. They include winery owners, wine writers, sommeliers, restaurateurs, importers and retailers and winemakers. Reflecting on her time as chair, Sarah Jane Evans MW said that it had been a “great honour” to hold the position of Chair of the Gran Orden.  “I am the first woman to have held the post, and I am sure I won’t be the last,” she said. “It has been a huge privilege to follow in the line of my outstanding predecessors.” During her time as chair, the committee welcomed exceptional new Caballeros: and launched a mentorship programme which highlights the new stars of the Spanish wine trade. “I am delighted to be handing the Gran Orden over to our new Chair in such good order and at such a good moment,” she added. 

Next steps for Spain

Atkin was recently awarded the Officer’s Cross of the Royal Order of Isabella the Catholic, an honour bestowed on Jane Evans back in 2024. He was invested into the Gran Orden in 2007 and is also a Chevalier du Tastevin; a member of the Ordre du Bontemps in Bordeaux and the Cofradía de Rioja.  The writer and photographer joined the rest of the Caballeros in thanking Sarah Jane for her years of service at the helm of the organisation.  He said: "Sarah Jane Evans has done an enormous amount for Spain and the Caballeros del Vino, so hers will be big shoes to fill. In the week that Spain has won the World Cup, I am proud to be her successor.  “Spain is the most exciting wine producing country in the world at the moment. "As the next chair of the Caballeros, I look forward to working alongside ICEX, the ambassador Emma Aparici and all of my fellow Caballeros to promote everything that is best about Spain."

title: New tricks: the changing face of Bordeaux
summary:
Forget the clichés and preconceptions: Bordeaux today is a hotbed of innovation, with a wealth of groundbreaking projects and initiatives under way.

The post New tricks: the changing face of Bordeaux appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Looming over Bordeaux’s harbour is the Cité du Vin, a swirling spaceship of a building designed by French XTU Agency architects Anouk Legendre and Nicolas Desmazières to mimic both the flow of the Garonne river and the swirling of wine in a skyscraper-sized glass. For centuries, Bordeaux has been synonymous with the expensive red wines that have built a world-class fine wine empire. But as the southwestern region of France adapts to a global drop in alcohol consumption and shifting consumer habits, a swathe of châteaux are showing a new side to Bordeaux. As symbolised by the wine museum’s architecture of flowing wine and the fast-moving Garonne, Bordeaux never sits still. It’s a region defined not by Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot and Cabernet Franc, but by deftness, diversity and the ability to swiftly adapt.

A fresh look

As consumers pivot towards lighter, fresher wines, dry white from Bordeaux is emerging as one of the region’s most dynamic and commercially important categories. While white grapes account for only 12% of vineyard plantings, producers are expanding and diversifying their offer, from crisp, fruit-driven cuvées and structured dry whites to elegant Crémants and lush vins liquoreux. Château Clarke was one of the first producers in the Médoc to make white wine back in 1885, with its first vintage in 1898. The wine, Le Merle Blanc de Château Clarke, was proudly revived by Baron Edmond de Rothschild 100 years later in 1998, after he acquired the land two decades earlier. Named after an oxymoronic ‘white blackbird’, marking a rare gem, the wine is crafted from the only four white grape varieties authorised by the Bordeaux AOC. In the cellar, extended lees ageing and precise bâtonnage balance freshness and complexity, showcasing the suitability of the Médoc’s warm, gravelly terroir for white winemaking. Among its fans is db Bordeaux correspondent Colin Hay, who waxed lyrical about the 2024 wine’s “expressive and lifted aromatic profile”, finding “distinct chalky-stony minerality” and white pear, quince, lime and green tea. “It is performing very well in terms of sales,” says the winery’s head of media relations, Marine Babion. “As an allocated cuvée where demand outstrips supply, this puts us in a comfortable position to consider expanding production.” And they’re not the only ones. From Fronsac to Côtes de Bourg, producers are following Clarke’s lead by investing in white wines to meet growing demand. Among them is Romain Baillou, owner of Château Le Piat, who planted Sauvignon Blanc and Muscadelle in 2024 – a bold move in the predominantly red wine Côtes de Bourg appellation – predicting strong potential for producing fresh, aromatic whites from the limestone soils.

Noble revamp

Yet dry styles are only part of Bordeaux’s white wine canvas. Noble rot is also undergoing a revamp, proving the region’s sweet wines can be modern, gastronomic and highly relevant today. Château Arche, nestled next to the stony, sweet village of Sauternes, with the pinnacle of Saint-Pierre-ès-Liens church visible from the vines, is tapping into France’s cocktail craze by showing why Sauternes should be a mixology staple. “The demand for Sauternes in cocktails comes from consumers and a new wave of mixologists who are not afraid to shake up traditional Sauternes consumption,” says commercial director Damien Cressot. Last October, the estate joined forces with Bordeaux’s Nektar bar for a week-long programme and masterclass dedicated to Sauternes-based cocktails. Such initiatives, combined with refreshed packaging and a more defined media strategy, are helping Sauternes to unlock a new generation of consumers. Just a short drive away, you’ll reach the lush vineyards of Château La Rame, one of the oldest properties in the appellation of Sainte-Croix-du-Mont, where wines are produced from vineyards overlooking the Garonne with subsoils containing thousands of fossilised oysters. Grown on clay-limestone soils, La Rame’s sweet wine, Gourmandise, is made from 100% botrytised, hand-harvested Sémillon, and comes with a more accessible price tag than Sauternes. “For us, Gourmandise shows that Bordeaux sweet wines can be repositioned beyond dessert as wines for aperitif moments, seafood, lobster, spicy cuisine, blue cheeses, foie gras and fruit-based desserts,” says co-owner Olivier Allo. What these producers show is that Bordeaux sweet wine can shine on both the back bar and the dinner table, catering to consumers who are increasingly seeking out new drinking occasions. Allo says that, while his wine is a heritage wine, it’s one that meets the expectations of today’s consumers, who are looking for “freshness, authenticity and accessibility”.

Red revolution

Red wine too is undergoing a revolution. It’s true that there will always be room for the rock star grands crus in Bordeaux, but now a new spate of lighter, fresher reds is gaining momentum, opening up a diverse range of drinkers and occasions, with a new definition for Bordeaux Claret surfing on the trend. This indicates that Bordeaux reds can still be ageworthy, but they can also be vibrant, aromatic and effortlessly drinkable. There’s room for every bottle, and a bottle for every occasion, whether that be on a Michelin restaurant wine list or around the pool with friends. Few embody this balance of tradition and innovation better than bright-eyed brothers Max and Alexander Sichel, representing the seventh generation of historic winery Maison Sichel, founded in 1886. Named newcomer of the year in 2025, Alexander is helping to redefine perceptions of Bordeaux through wines such as Ocha, named after the Japanese word for tea due to its delicate, aromatic character. Produced using a short maceration and whole-bunch fermentation, the wine has found a following in Bordeaux’s bars and among high-end UK restaurants, including Mountain and Brunswick House. “They all loved it,” says Alexander. “It’s the new face of Bordeaux.” While Alexander gets busy wooing a younger generation of drinkers with lighter, contemporary styles, Max is championing Bordeaux’s classics. His forthcoming project, César, aims to make some of the region’s most acclaimed wines more accessible by bringing together each year’s most awarded Bordeaux bottlings under a single label at an affordable price point. The initiative is designed to spotlight Bordeaux’s quality credentials while appealing to consumers who are drinking less, but seeking better value in the wines they choose.

Trailblazers

Bordeaux is not short of trailblazing châteaux, from the multi-coloured wines of Pessac-Léognan’s Château Picque Caillou and Marjosse in Entre-Deux-Mers to the silky, fruit-forward reds of Saint Emilion’s Cantenac. But there’s one name that the conversation isn’t complete without – and that’s Olivier Cazenave. Branded as Bordeaux’s “most radical winemaker” by Naked Wines, when he’s not in the vineyard, Cazenave can be found dodging fish while surfing on the breezy stretch of the Dordogne river in his backyard at Château de Bel. The former literature student’s bouquet of bottles is zesty and eclectic, spanning solera-style Cabernet Franc, easy-drinking claret and single varietals. He vows to make wines that are “proudly accessible – each of them ready to drink but equally ready to evolve”. For the winemaker, Bordeaux’s future lies in its ability to innovate while maintaining its identity. That looks like letting the vineyard lead the way: “Sometimes it must be a single grape, sometimes not; sometimes it must be full-bodied, sometimes not,” says Cazenave.

Respect for the land

And letting the vineyard lead includes respect for the land. Cazenave’s wines have been certified biodynamic since 2016, pointing to a broader movement making waves across Bordeaux. Over the past decade, the region’s winemakers have made significant strides in sustainability, positioning Bordeaux among the world’s leading wine regions in balancing viticultural excellence with the realities of climate change. Today, more than 75% of Bordeaux vineyards are certified under an environmental scheme, while the organic vineyard area has expanded rapidly in recent years. This spans biodiversity initiatives and reduced chemical inputs, plus water management and carbon reduction strategies. Winemakers are experimenting with new grape varieties, allowing limited plantings and blending to test their suitability for future conditions. Alongside this, they are adapting rootstocks, improving soil management, using cover crops and mapping mesoclimates to identify cooler areas. A key focus is developing new, disease-resistant, late-ripening grape varieties that could cut pesticide use by up to 90% while also helping vineyards adapt to climate change. Vignobles Gabriel & Co., a collective of 34 winegrowers committed to ethical wine, became the first French wine sector operator to obtain Fair for Life certification in September 2020. The accreditation supports fair trade and sustainable farming practices. “This was an important milestone, both for our company and for the wider Bordeaux wine industry, as it demonstrated that a fair trade approach could be successfully applied to a collective of independent winegrowers in France,” says Pauline Réaud, the group’s marketing and communications officer. For Réaud, it represents more than a certification. “It is the recognition of our values and of a different way of doing business in Bordeaux,” she explains. But even those without certified organic status are employing unique and innovative methods to make sustainability a priority. At George 7, located in the Fronsac sub-region, English winemaker Sally Evans feeds olive skins from oil production to flies, then uses the poo as fertiliser to help vines fight mildew – a sustainable alternative to manure. At Château Clarke, sheep are deployed in the vineyard in spring to beat frost, successfully raising the temperature this year by 2o C. Château d’Arche has planted thousands of trees to host local wildlife such as bats and birds, reducing insect pests without chemicals. For years, Bordeaux has been saddled with a reputation that feels increasingly out of step with the explosive innovations on the ground. Beyond the clichés lies a region producing everything from crisp whites and cocktail-friendly sweet wines to lighter reds, while leading the charge on sustainability without losing sight of its heritage. As Cazenave urges: “Change your mind about Bordeaux. Don’t be like sheep.” Unless, of course, that is the sheep sleepily raising the temperatures at Château Clarke on cool spring mornings, cocooning the vines from frostbite – tranquil mascots for the beacon of Bordeaux’s brilliance.

title: 10 reasons why Moldovan wine should be on your radar
summary:
The story of Moldovan wine is one of fortitude in facing great challenges – and now this remarkable wine nation is ready to take its place on the global stage. We pick out 10 reasons why the country is worth a closer look by those in the drinks trade.

The post 10 reasons why Moldovan wine should be on your radar appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The story of Moldovan wine is one of fortitude in facing great challenges – and now this remarkable wine nation is ready to take its place on the global stage. We pick out 10 reasons why the country is worth a closer look by those in the drinks trade. FOR A country of only 2.3 million people – a population similar to that of a small European city such as Vienna – Moldova punches with remarkable force in the world of wine. It has the highest density of vineyards on the planet. It sits on some of the world’s most fertile soils. It harbours a network of underground cellars so vast that you can drive through them. And it is home to a family of native grape varieties that have no real equivalent anywhere else on Earth. Yet Moldova remains largely absent from the conversation in UK wine retail and trade media. That may be about to change. And db has picked out 10 reasons why.

1. A STRONG NATIONAL WINE CULTURE

Moldova’s 114,000 hectares of vineyards – including those used for home production and table grapes – represent a huge proportion of its total land area for a country this size. At its production peak during the Soviet era, that figure reached 350,000ha. Even now, reduced to roughly one-third of its former extent, Moldova ranks first globally in terms of vineyard density. With grape vines covering roughly 7% of the country’s agricultural land, there are 4ha of vineyards for every 100 people. The wine industry accounts for approximately 3% of Moldova’s GDP and more than 8% of the country’s exports, with around 4% of the economically-active population being directly or indirectly involved in the sector. The country is sometimes described as being shaped like a bunch of grapes, which is apt considering that viticulture is not merely a means of income in Moldova, but a defining feature of national identity. Stefan Iamandi, director of the National Office of Vine and Wine, puts it succinctly: “For us, wine is not an industry – it is all around us; it is part of our culture.”

2. IDEAL GRAPE GROWING CONDITIONS

Beneath Moldova’s vineyards lies chernozem – the famously rich black soil that covers only 1.8% of the Earth’s surface, and which is concentrated almost entirely in Moldova and Ukraine. The agricultural productivity this enables is remarkable: Moldova is the largest exporter of walnuts in the European Union and produces outstanding fruit and vegetables. For viticulture, yields of 8–12 tonnes per hectare are achievable with relative ease – requiring discipline in the vineyard, but also testament to the extraordinary natural fertility of the land. Couple this with a continental climate offering diurnal temperature shifts of up to 20o C, warm summers and river valley sites along the Nistru, Prut and Raut, and the conditions for quality winemaking are clearly present. Furthermore, a mixture of forests, grasslands and riparian ecosystems enhance biodiversity in the wine estates, while a limestone subsoil regulates water availability for the vines, and sloping landscapes improve airflow and reduce frost risk.

3. RECORD-BREAKING WINE COLLECTIONS

Forget climate-controlled warehouses. Moldova’s flagship cellars are former limestone quarries that stretch for tens of kilometres underground – and you can drive through them. Cricova, founded in 1952 and Moldova’s largest sparkling wine producer, has 70km of galleries. Mileștii Mici, just 10km from the capital Chișinău, holds the Guinness World Record for the largest wine collection, with more than 1.5 million bottles stored in over 200km of driveable tunnels. Among them is a bottle of Jerusalem Easter wine from 1902 – reportedly the only one in existence. Both cellars are state-owned and have become significant wine tourism destinations in their own right. Furthermore, they provide the perfect conditions for the slow maturation of Moldova’s fine and affordable traditional method sparkling wines.

4. A ‘GIRLISH’ NATIVE GRAPE FAMILY

Perhaps Moldova’s most intriguing viticultural asset is the Fetească family – a group of indigenous varieties whose name translates, roughly, as “girlish” or “for girls”, following a regional tradition of naming grape varieties with feminine associations. There are three main members: Fetească Albă (white), Fetească Regală (royal), and Fetească Neagră (black). Together they function rather like the Pinot family – related, distinctive, each with its own personality, and all requiring careful handling in the vineyard and winery. Fetească Albă is the gentlest of the three: floral, citrusy and easy-drinking, often compared to Pinot Grigio in its aromatic lightness and bright acidity. Fetească Regală, the youngest of the group, leans toward elderflower and is increasingly being explored in barrel-aged expressions. Fetească Neagră, the red member – sometimes referred to as the ‘black maiden’ – produces wines of impressive depth: black fruit, dried plum, dark chocolate and firm but fine tannin. At its best, there is a Malbec-like intensity to it, though with a distinctly Eastern European character. At Salcuța in the Stefan Vodă region, marketing director Dina Pislaru is experimenting with Fetească Albă in new French oak; at Purcari, there is a Fetească Albă varietal traditional method sparkling wine. The potential of this family is still being explored.

5. AN ANCIENT AND RARE VARIETY

Rară Neagră – meaning ‘rare black’ – is believed by some specialists to be among the oldest cultivated grape varieties in the world, with traces of viticulture in the region dating to around 3,000 BC or earlier.
Though related varieties are also found in Romania (where it is known as Băbească Neagră), it is in Moldova – and particularly in the southeastern Stefan Vodă PGI – that the grape has found its most compelling modern expression. Like Pinot Noir, which it loosely resembles in weight and character, Rară Neagră is difficult and expensive to grow, with uneven ripening across the same cluster requiring multiple passes through the vineyard. Purcari, which has done most to rescue and promote the variety, has established a nursery to study all available clones. In the glass, Rară Neagră typically offers ruby colour, a medium to light body, fine tannins and a sour cherry note alongside redcurrant, wild blackberry and varying degrees of barrel influence, depending on the winemaking. It’s a fresh, gently fruity, food-friendly wine that’s on-trend for those seeking lighter reds and good-value alternatives to Pinot Noir.

6. A WHITE GRAPE FROM TWO REDS

Viorica – named after both an Alpine flower (Scilla bifolia) and, reportedly, the wife of the Soviet-era scientist who created it – is a crossing of two red varieties that produces a distinctive white wine. It combines Seibel 13-666 (a French hybrid used for its resilience) and Aleatico (an aromatic Italian red Muscat). Developed at Moldova’s wine research institute in 1969, during a period of experimentation aimed at producing frost-resistant varieties, it is now one of Moldova’s key white grapes. The paradox of its parentage is matched by the character of the wine: intensely floral and aromatic, with notes of white flowers, grapey-Muscaty richness and a dry, fresh finish underpinned by fine phenolics. Purcari’s Remus Turcan describes it as one of Moldova’s “signature grapes”. Whether its pronounced aromatic profile has broad mainstream appeal is debated – it pairs particularly well with Asian cuisine – but as a talking point and a genuine expression of place, it has few rivals in the category.

7. ALIGOTÉ AND MOLDOVA’S ANSWER TO COGNAC

Divin – Moldova’s aged grape brandy – has been produced in the country since 1896, when the first distillery was established: Calarasi Divin. Today, it holds a protected geographical indication that is one of the strictest in the world, with rigorous regulations similar to famous appellations such as Cognac and Armagnac. Produced throughout Moldova and aged in oak, Divin ranges from youthful three-year expressions, with their fiery, floral character, to magnificent 25-year-old bottlings of extraordinary complexity, with flavours of dark chocolate, molasses, prunes, white pepper and an appealing smoothness. Protected as a geographical indication (GI) since 2012, Divin – which means ‘divine’, as well as ‘from the vine’ – is produced from varieties chosen specifically for distillation, in particular Aligoté, which explains why this is Moldova’s most planted white grape. Other widely employed grapes for Divin include Georgia’s Rkatsiteli, and Moldova’s native Fetească Albă. One of the top three producers is Bardar, where the distillery produces a five-year-old expression that sells in its home market for around €6 for a 500ml bottle, which highlights the value-for-money proposition of this crowd-pleasing brandy.

8. A MODERN VINOUS REINVENTION

In 2006, Russia – then the destination for nearly 90% of Moldovan wine exports – imposed a total ban on Moldovan wine imports, ostensibly on phytosanitary grounds, but widely interpreted as political pressure connected to Russia’s ambitions to “federalise” Moldova. Overnight, 60 million bottles became zero. Purcari alone lost the equivalent of around €2m-worth of stock, some of which was reportedly bulldozed. Dozens of companies went under. It was, paradoxically, the catalyst the Moldovan wine industry needed. Forced to modernise, diversify and compete on quality rather than volume, producers invested in new equipment, planted higher-density vineyards and began to explore international markets in earnest. The National Office of Vine and Wine was established in 2013, creating a system of protected geographical indications modelled on the EU framework – with guarantees on tracibility, along with controls on yields and rules on grape varieties, as well as independent quality controls. Codru, Stefan Vodă and Valul lui Traian are the main wine regions, with Divin as the brandy GI. A public-private partnership, funded by a levy of around one cent per litre on wine production (generating roughly €4m annually), now manages a marketing and quality programme that has made Moldova the most award-winning wine country in Eastern Europe by some measures, with around 60% gold medal rates at international competitions. From near-total dependence on Russia, exports to Europe are now 60% of total volume; Russia accounts for just 3%.

9. A STRONG FAITH IN SWEET WINES

Among Moldova’s more singular wine traditions is a sweet, iron-rich red wine – containing 16% ABV and 160g/l residual sugar (16%), explaining why it is made to a rule known as just ‘16-16’. Called Pastoral, it is made from Cabernet Sauvignon, Malbec and other varieties, and traditionally blessed in church at Easter before being taken home for family consumption. Historically it has been considered health-giving, and has been associated with use by Soviet-era cosmonauts, even though drinking alcohol in orbit was officially forbidden. It pairs, apparently, with babaneagra – ‘black old lady’ – a traditional baked pudding made with milk, eggs, sugar and baking soda. This fortified style is just one example of a wine culture that is genuinely, unselfconsciously rooted in daily life in a way that is rare, even in a European wine-producing nation. And it’s not the country’s only richly sweet drink: such is the severity of Moldova’s winters, the country is able to craft icewine too, using aromatic local grapes like Viorica, as well as international varieties like Riesling – and even red grape Cabernet Sauvignon.

10. A GLOBAL SUCCESS STORY

The story of modern Moldovan wine is, at its core, a story of reinvention under pressure. The Soviet anti-alcohol campaign of 1987 saw tens of thousands of hectares of vineyards grubbed up – including a disproportionate amount of the oldest indigenous varieties, which were far from the source of the drinking problem. The Russian embargo of 2006 to 2012 destroyed the export model overnight. The war in Ukraine closed the port of Odessa, adding 330 kilometres and around 30 cents per litre to bulk shipping costs from Stefan Vodă, with wine now having to go via the alternative port of Constanța in Romania. And yet, Moldova now exports to 70 countries. Japan has tripled its imports in three years. Nigeria and Ghana are emerging markets with real momentum. Romania – historically and culturally close – takes US$40m-worth of Moldovan wine annually, making it the top export destination. A younger generation of winemakers is returning to indigenous varieties, launching boutique labels and telling a story that is finding receptive ears in trade markets. There are now 250 wineries in Moldova, compared to around 60 in Romania, a country many times its size. Thirty new producers opened in the past two years alone. The stork, which nests in Moldova in large numbers, features on the official seal of the national wine body. As a bird that signifies new beginnings, it seems an especially appropriate symbol for an emergent wine industry that has survived empire, occupation, embargo and war.

Moldova: key facts

• Ranked number one in the world by vineyard density • Total surface area of vineyards: 114,000 hectares • Commercial wine-growing vineyards: 67,400ha • White wine grapes: 34,100ha • Red grapes: 33,300ha

Most planted whites:

Aligoté Sauvignon Blanc Chardonnay Muscat Ottonel Pinot Gris/Blanc Fetească Albă/Regală

Most planted reds:

Cabernet Sauvignon Merlot Pinot Noir Saperavi Rară Neagră Fetească Neagră

Three wine regions:

Codru Stefan Vodă Valul lui Traian

GI for brandy production:

Divin

Exports & wine production:

Approx. 200 million litres Exports to 75 countries, with 60% of production going to the EU

title: Harvest begins in Roussillon
summary:
Domaine Lafage has begun its earliest-ever harvest following an exceptionally early growing season in Roussillon.

The post Harvest begins in Roussillon appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Domaine Lafage has begun its earliest-ever harvest following an exceptionally early growing season in Roussillon.

Among the first estates in the region to start harvesting each year, Domaine Lafage started picking today (Thursday 23 July).

The unusually early start to the 2026 harvest has been driven by a combination of factors. Budburst occurred around 15 days earlier than normal, accelerating the growing season, while generous rainfall early in the year replenished water reserves. This was followed by an exceptional weather pattern, with consistently high temperatures throughout spring and summer and extreme heat recorded from May onwards.

The first grapes to be picked are Muscat Petits Grains from vineyards along the Mediterranean coast. Harvested at optimal ripeness, they will be used to produce the Lafage family's lightly sparkling white wine, Lafabuleuse, which is once again expected to have a moderate alcohol level of around 9% ABV.

A pioneer of regenerative viticulture, Domaine Lafage has had its entire vineyard estate certified by A Greener World (AGW) since the beginning of 2026. Over several years, the Lafage family has developed a range of practices aimed at improving vineyard resilience to climate change while enhancing biodiversity across its diverse terroirs. These include innovative water management strategies, soil regeneration through compost and biochar applications, and agroforestry initiatives, all designed to create healthier, more resilient vineyard ecosystems.

Following several vintages affected by severe drought, the 2026 season has brought renewed optimism to Roussillon. Winter and spring rainfall has helped restore balance to the vineyards, creating favourable conditions for a healthy, high-quality harvest with strong potential.

title: Champagne to limit production to 250m bottles for 2026
summary:
The region of Champagne is to cap the amount of wine that can be made from this year’s harvest at 8,800kg/ha ­– the equivalent of around 250 million bottles.

The post Champagne to limit production to 250m bottles for 2026 appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The region of Champagne is to cap the amount of wine that can be made from this year’s harvest at 8,800kg/ha ­– the equivalent of around 250 million bottles.

Set by the Comité Champagne (CIVC), the figure represents the “rendement commercialisable” or “marketable yield”, and this year’s limit is the lowest in the modern era of the appellation, with the exception of 2020’s Covid-induced anomaly – when the region capped production at 8,000kg/ha (representing fewer than 230m bottles). The limit for this year’s harvest is lower than in 2025 (9,000kg/ha), as well as 2024, when it was capped at 10,000kg/ha, which, in turn, was down a little more than 12% on the 2023 vintage, when it was set at 11,400kg/ha (and almost 17% lower than in 2022, when it was 12,000kg/ha).

A 'collective' decision

Justifying the decision to set this figure, which comes after cuts in the three preceding harvests, the heads of the Comité Champagne stressed that the limit was set by the grape growers and winemakers to prevent the region from producing more wine than it can sell. In a statement released by the Comité Champagne yesterday, it stated, “This collective decision aims to gradually rebalance stock, while preserving the economic viability of the vineyards and maintaining quality standards.” Commenting on demand for the sparkling wine, it recorded a 1.2% growth in shipments for the first half of this year, with a total of 107.1 million bottles from January to June, with exports driving the increase. Should that trend continue, the region would ship 269m bottles by the year-end, up on last year's total of 266m, which itself was a 2% decrease compared with 271m bottles in 2024. “Champagne has a unique collective model that allows it to adapt its decisions to market realities without losing sight of what matters most: preserving the appellation’s value over the long term,” said David Chatillon, president of the Union des Maisons de Champagne and co-president of the Comité Champagne Continuing he said, “In an economic climate that remains uncertain, and as the industry continues to gradually rebalance its stock, this yield reflects a responsible approach.”

A complex harvest

Also commenting on the figure, Maxime Toubart, president of the Syndicat Général des Vignerons de la Champagne and co-president of the Comité Champagne, said, “The yield set this year reflects a measured decision, mindful of both the reality of the vineyards and the future of the industry.” In terms of harvest conditions, the growing season was described as “complex”, marked by episodes of frost, extreme heat in June and the recent onset of drought, with significant variation seen across Champagne's vineyards. It is expected that the harvest will take place between 20 and 25 August on average – which is 10 to 15 days earlier than usual – with the official start of the harvest is scheduled for 15 August. As previously reported by db, following an unusually early and heavily frost-damaged start to the season, followed by recent heatwave conditions, Champagne could be on course for the earliest start date for harvesting in its long history.

Read more

Everything you need to know about the current Champagne yield Champagne restricts yields to lowest level since Covid

title: Leopard attacks Rajasthan liquor store cashier
summary:
A leopard prowled into a liquor store in Rajasthan, northwestern India, and proceeded to pounce on a panicked shopkeeper before roaming around the store, knocking over bottles of booze, CCTV footage shows.

The post Leopard attacks Rajasthan liquor store cashier appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
A leopard prowled into a liquor store in Rajasthan, northwestern India, and proceeded to pounce on a panicked shopkeeper before roaming around the store, knocking over bottles of booze, CCTV footage shows. Colleagues in the shop in Todaraisingh, Rajasthan can be heard shrieking in the dramatic video footage, before the wild cat suddenly lunges at 25-year-old worker, Sanjay Gurjar.  The cashier is seen wrestling with the fierce feline, before he manages to push it off, leap over the table, and flee. The leopard then moves through the store, knocking over bottles of spirits. Just moments before entering the shop the animal reportedly attacked bystander Fateh Lal Koli, 40, while he was sitting outside the alcohol store.

Aftermath of the attack

Both victims of the attack are now receiving treatment for their injuries in hospital. The feline, thought to be a five-year-old male, was tranquilised by a specialist animal unit, who are set to perform medical checks before releasing him back into the wild. While many leopards live in Todaraising’s surrounding forests, they usually avoid human contact unless injured or threatened.

title: Diageo’s India unit reports 52% leap in quarterly profit
summary:
United Spirits, Diageo's Indian subsidiary, has reported a sharp rise in first-quarter profit, fuelled by robust demand for its premium spirits portfolio.

The post Diageo’s India unit reports 52% leap in quarterly profit appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
United Spirits, Diageo's Indian subsidiary, has reported a sharp rise in first-quarter profit, fuelled by robust demand for its premium spirits portfolio. The spirits maker and distributor of brands including Johnnie Walker, Antiquity, Black & White whisky and Tanqueray gin said standalone net profit rose 51.6% to 3.91bn rupees (£30.27m) in the three months to 30 June, up from 2.58bn rupees (£19.98m) rupees a year earlier. Revenue for the quarter increased 5% year-on-year to 61.13bn rupees, while operating expenses rose 4.6%. Growth was led by the company's premium portfolio, with net sales in the segment rising 10.1% from a year earlier. Premium brands accounted for 91.7% of United Spirits' net sales during the quarter. Commenting on the company's performance, Praveen Someshwar, CEO and managing director, United Spirits, said, "We have commenced fiscal 2027 on a strong note with double-digit growth in the Prestige & Above segment. "Our consumer centric interventions give us confidence to increase growth further as the year progresses. We continue to future-proof our portfolio while creating enduring value for all our stakeholders."

Karnataka reforms

Analysts at Jefferies had expected the company to benefit from improved performance of its McDowell's brand following a relaunch, as well as recent tax changes in the southern Indian state of Karnataka. Karnataka, one of India's largest alcohol markets, announced in March that it would abolish government price controls and introduce a strength-based excise taxation system from April. The changes, which took effect on 11 May, allow alcohol producers greater flexibility to set prices while reducing tax rates on premium spirits. The reforms are expected to support demand for higher-end liquor brands in the state.

title: Could craft beer be seeing the start of a second wave?
summary:
In the US, craft brewing trends have held steady, declines in operating breweries have slightly increased and consumer engagement has strengthened. Jessica Mason asks: are we seeing craft beer’s resurgence?

The post Could craft beer be seeing the start of a second wave? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
In the US, craft brewing trends have held steady, declines in operating breweries have slightly increased and consumer engagement has strengthened. Jessica Mason asks: are we seeing craft beer’s resurgence? The Brewers Association (BA) released results from its midyear survey, presenting an updated look at the independent beer sector in the US. Despite the findings still pointing to continued pressure, there are however several indicators to suggest the contraction may be moderating. Among the analysis, the BA estimates craft volume declined 4% in the first six months of 2026, compared to the same period in 2025. But the overall decline of craft volume is not the only story in the data, because, looking more closely, it was identified that among survey respondents, 54% reported growth, 43% reported declines, and 3% remained flat.

Seeing a shift

In fact, every craft brewery type had a greater share reporting growth than decline, with taprooms (57% growing) and regional breweries (56% growing) leading the charge. Among breweries producing more than 10,000 barrels, 59% reported growth and just 40% reported declines. While the BA admitted that there’s likely positive-leaning sample bias since breweries are more likely to report when things are going well, this is still an overall bump from 49% reporting growth in the same survey last year. Speaking to the drinks business, Brewers Association staff economist Matt Gacioch said: "It's too early to say craft beer has definitively turned a corner, but trends tend to steady out before they shift direction. The data suggests that this may be where the industry finds itself today." Craft consumers offered another encouraging signal. In the 2026 BA and Harris Poll Consumer Survey of more than 2,000 adults, monthly craft consumption among craft drinkers reached 85%, up 10 percentage points year-over-year and up to its highest level since 2019–2020. Craft drinkers also visited breweries 5.5 times on average over the past year, up from 5.1 in 2025.

'Cautious optimism'

Within the report, the BA remained cautious when approaching the possibility of a turnaround with too much hope, but the initial signs are looking positive. Instead of giving a completely optimistic view, Gacioch tentatively said that “in spite of these bright spots, it is too soon to say whether craft has reached the bottom of the valley for trends in production volume or number of breweries”. But the data should give brewers a lift, especially since there are many out there who have been battered by the onslaught of headwinds the sector has faced. As Gacioch explained: “Still, after several years of figures moving in the wrong direction, that moderation may catalyse some cautious optimism for an industry of perseverant brewers.” Gacioch admitted that “the world in which craft brewers operate hasn’t gotten any easier or more predictable in 2026, but perhaps the breweries that have weathered the storm so far are the ones that have best positioned themselves for storm weathering into the future”. Granted, the number of operating breweries also continued to decline. For instance, there were 9,344 breweries operating in June 2026, down 1.8% from 9,515 a year earlier in June 2025. This represents a slight uptick in the rate of decline, from -1% in the first half of 2025. The number of regional breweries and microbreweries each declined 3%, followed by taprooms at 2% and brewpubs at 1%. However, because taprooms and brewpubs are far more numerous, the BA pointed out that those smaller percentage changes still represent meaningful absolute numbers of closures.

Breweries with taprooms were best-performing

For the first half of 2026, taprooms were the best-performing brewery type by volume change, outpacing other models. Additionally, when assessing by channel, distributed draught beat distributed packaged products and onsite sales. The BA noted that this suggests craft brewers are finding opportunities to meet consumer needs in draught amid wholesaler consolidation and portfolio rationalisation. Even though retail scan data showed steeper declines than the BA estimate, for instance, off-trade data from NielsenIQ (NIQ) has shown BA-defined craft down 5.2% in the first half of 2026. By comparison, NIQ-defined craft was down 5.6% for the same period, and beer plus non-alcohol beer was down 4%. Looking at the bigger picture, there is still a long journey ahead. After all, beer continues to experience declines alongside other beverage alcohol categories. This can be seen from Wine & Spirits Wholesalers of America (WSWA) SipSource data which showed spirits volume down 5.3% and wine down 9.2% for the 12 months ending May 2026. As the report flagged, even though that period does not align perfectly with the first-half craft beer figures, it does offer the most recent comparison available.
